content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
August 17,
1992
Honorable Sandra Benitez
Riverbank City Council
City of Riverbank
5707 Third Street
Riverbank,
CA
95367
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
G-92—403
Dear Councilmember Beriitez:
This is in response to your letter requesting advice
concerning an unnamed county supervisor regard the supervisor’s
responsibilities under the conflict-of-interest provisions of the
Political Reform Act
(the “Act”).
1
As discussed in our telephone conversation of July 16,
1992,
the Act requires the Commission to provide formal written advice
to any person whose duties under the Act are in auestion or to
that person’s authorized representative.
(Section 83114(b).)
The
Commission does not provide advice to third parties concerning
another person’s duties under the Act.
(Regulation 18329(b) (8),
copy enclosed.)
It does not appear that you have the
authorization of the supervisor in question to request advice from
the Commission at this time.
Consequently,
we are unable to
provide advice on the issues you raised in your letter.
Generally speaking,
however,
for a conflict of interest to
exist under the Act,
an official must have an economic interest in
a specific decision which will be foreseeably and materially
affected.
2
Section 87103 provides:
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.
All references to Regulations are to Title 2,
-ivision
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Officials should also consider the appearance of bias in a
decision.
However, the Act does not require disqualification
based solely on appearances of bias.
428 J Street, Suite 800 •
P.O. Box 807 •
Sacramento, CA
95812 •
(916) 322-5660


Our File No.
G-92-403
Page
2
An official has a financial interest in a
decision within the meaning of Section 87100 if it
is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will
have a material financial effect, distinguishable
from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family
or on:
(a)
Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(b)
Any real property in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status,
aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars
($250)
or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within
12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
(d)
Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee,
or holds any position of management.
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or
agent for a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to, received by,
or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.
For purposes of this section,
indirect
investment or interest means any investment or
interest owned by the spouse or dependent child of
a public official,
by an agent on behalf of a
public official, or by a business entity or trust
in which the official, the official’s agents,
spouse,
and dependent children own directly,
indirectly,
or beneficially a 10-percent interest
or greater.
Absent an economic interest in a decision,
the Act does not
require disqualification.
If you believe that the supervisor has
an economic interest in a decision in which the supervisor has


Our File No.
G—92—403
Page
3
participated,
you may wish to file a complaint with the
Commission’s Enforcement Division.
A complaint form has been
enclosed for your convenience.
We have also enclosed the following materials for your
general information:
(1)
The Political Reform Act of 1974 as amended to
January 1,
1991.
(2)
A Guide to The Political Reform Act of 1974.
(3)
A series of conflict-of-interest outlines.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322-59Ol.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel
By:
Luisa Menchaca
Counsel,
Legal Division
SH:LM:aa
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of lOc per page.

sections.question_synthetic: Does an unnamed county supervisor have conflict-of-interest duties under the Political Reform Act regarding a specific decision?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot provide advice on this matter because the requestor lacks authorization from the supervisor in question. The FPPC only provides formal written advice to persons whose duties are in question or their authorized representatives, not to third parties. However, the FPPC provides general guidance that a conflict of interest exists only when an official has an economic interest in a specific decision that will be foreseeably and materially affected.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Declination letter with general legal guidance provided despite inability to advise on specific case. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections present; guidance extracted from body text. Document includes detailed statutory provisions regarding financial interests and conflict definitions.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does an unnamed county supervisor have conflict-of-interest duties under the Political Reform Act regarding a specific decision?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC cannot provide advice on this matter because the requestor lacks authorization from the supervisor in question. The FPPC only provides formal written advice to persons whose duties are in question or their authorized representatives, not to third parties. However, the FPPC provides general guidance that a conflict of interest exists only when an official has an economic interest in a specific decision that will be foreseeably and materially affected.

embedding.summary: FPPC declining to provide informal advice to Councilmember Benitez regarding an unnamed county supervisor's conflict-of-interest responsibilities, as the requestor lacks the supervisor's authorization. The Commission provides general information about when conflicts of interest exist under the Political Reform Act and suggests filing a complaint if a conflict is suspected.
